description: Initial static analysis of SFOS 22.0.0 GA installer ISO and SFLoader firmware
methodology: Firmware extraction from ISO -> LUKS decryption -> SFLoader initramfs extraction from bzImage vmlinux -> Script and binary analysis

findings.title: Hardcoded SFLoader Advanced Troubleshooting Shell Password
findings.description: The SFLoader pre-boot environment contains a hardcoded password 'tHe3mu8k3t33r8' that grants root shell access via the 'Advanced Troubleshoot' menu option. This password is identical across all Sophos hardware and virtual appliance models as the same SFLoader firmware is deployed universally. An attacker with physical console access, serial access, or remote console access (IPMI/iLO/iDRAC/vSphere) can obtain a root shell in the SFLoader environment, which has direct access to the appliance's disk, nvram, and firmware management tools.
findings.evidence: read_password1() function: if [ "$value" = "tHe3mu8k3t33r8" ]; then return 0; fi -- followed by call to advanced_troubleshoot() which executes /bin/sh as root
findings.impact: Root shell in SFLoader pre-boot environment. Attacker can: (1) read/write nvram including license and configuration data, (2) access dm-crypt encrypted root partition via loadfw, (3) install backdoored firmware, (4) extract all credentials from the appliance filesystem, (5) modify GRUB boot configuration. Requires physical/console access.
findings.remediation: Replace hardcoded password with per-device credential derived from hardware identity (e.g., serial number + TPM-sealed secret). Alternatively, require authentication against the SFOS credential store.
findings.notes: This is the SFLoader environment, NOT the full SFOS. The SFLoader runs when SFOS fails to boot or during firmware management. However, the shell has full root access and can interact with the installed SFOS filesystem.

findings.title: Hardcoded Installer and SFLoader Root Password (admin)
findings.description: Both the installer initrd and SFLoader initramfs use the same root password hash ($1$sfVey4VR$VOGIbS/Qlnn9.RGy8ot4K1) which cracks to 'admin' via MD5-crypt. This password is shared across all appliance models. While the installer and SFLoader environments are not directly network-accessible under normal operation, the weak password lowers the barrier for any attacker who obtains console access.
findings.evidence: root:$1$sfVey4VR$VOGIbS/Qlnn9.RGy8ot4K1:0:0:root:/tmp:/bin/sh -- cracked with john using rockyou.txt in <1 second
findings.impact: Low direct impact -- these environments are not network-accessible by default. However, combined with SFOS-001, demonstrates a pattern of weak/hardcoded credentials in the boot chain.
findings.remediation: Use a stronger, unique password or disable password authentication in favor of certificate-based or hardware-backed authentication.
findings.notes: Design Note per standing rules -- hardcoded credentials in a local-only installer environment. Documented for completeness but not a reportable finding on its own.

findings.title: Unauthenticated HTTP Firmware Upload Server (fwhttpd) in SFLoader
findings.category: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
findings.description: The SFLoader environment includes fwhttpd, a minimal HTTP server that accepts unauthenticated firmware file uploads via multipart POST to ./cgi-bin/uploadimg.cgi. The server is started by geturl.sh when an operator selects 'Upload' from the firmware loading menu. It listens on all interfaces with no authentication, no TLS, and no access controls. The uploaded file is written to /tmp/currentfw.img. While firmware is subsequently validated by nsgsig-verify/GPG, the upload endpoint itself processes untrusted multipart POST data with no apparent bounds checking.
findings.evidence: Strings analysis: 'uasge: %s [-f filepath, default "%s"] [-p port, default "%d"] [-d]', 'HTTP/1.1 200 OK', '<FORM ACTION="./cgi-bin/uploadimg.cgi" METHOD="POST" ENCTYPE="multipart/form-data">', 'boundry end is broken %d - %d', 'Content-Length:', '/tmp/currentfw.img'. No authentication-related strings found. Uses sscanf for parsing.
findings.impact: Attack vectors: (1) Buffer overflow in multipart boundary/Content-Length parsing (C binary using sscanf), (2) Unauthenticated firmware upload (firmware validation occurs post-upload -- crash during parsing bypasses validation), (3) Network-accessible from any interface when active. Requires SFLoader to be running and operator to have selected firmware upload mode.
findings.remediation: Add authentication to fwhttpd. Use TLS. Implement proper bounds checking on multipart parsing. Consider replacing custom C HTTP server with a hardened alternative.
findings.notes: NEEDS BINARY ANALYSIS -- fwhttpd is a ~15KB stripped C binary. Full disassembly needed to identify specific memory corruption vulnerabilities. The multipart boundary parsing ('boundry end is broken') and Content-Length handling via sscanf are prime targets.

findings.title: LUKS Encryption Key Stored in Plaintext on Installer Media
findings.category: CWE-321: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key
findings.description: The LUKS encryption key for the firmware installer payload (fwimgs.img) is stored as a plaintext 4096-byte binary file at /bin/usbkey within the installer initrd. This key is identical across all Sophos SFOS 22.0 installer media. Anyone with access to the ISO can extract the key and decrypt the firmware images, enabling offline analysis of all firmware components within the LUKS container.
findings.impact: Firmware confidentiality is not a strong security boundary. The LUKS layer protects firmware during distribution but does not prevent extraction by a motivated analyst. The actual root filesystem has a second dm-crypt layer with a derived key, which provides the real protection.
findings.remediation: If firmware confidentiality is a security goal, use a key derivation scheme tied to the target hardware rather than a static key shipped with the installer.
findings.notes: This is a firmware distribution design choice, not a vulnerability in the traditional sense. Documented because it enables further analysis.

findings.title: Firmware Signature Validation Logic Gap (nsgsig-verify Fallthrough)
findings.category: CWE-347: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
findings.description: The firmware validation flow in geturl.sh treats nsgsig-verify return codes 2 and 5 as non-fatal, falling through to legacy GPG validation. This dual-path validation creates a potential logic gap: if an attacker can craft firmware that causes nsgsig-verify to return code 2 or 5 (but is not a valid NSG-signed image), the firmware proceeds to GPG validation which may have different security properties. The specific meaning of error codes 2 and 5 from nsgsig-verify is unknown without further analysis of that binary.
findings.evidence: case "${error}" in 0) filepath=$1; return 0;; 2|5) ## break;; *) display_error; rm -f $1*; return 1;; esac -- error codes 2 and 5 fall through to GPG validation path
findings.impact: If GPG validation can be bypassed or if the GPG keyring is weaker than the NSG trust store, this fallthrough enables firmware tampering. Requires further analysis of nsgsig-verify error semantics and GPG keyring contents.
findings.remediation: Treat all non-zero nsgsig-verify return codes as fatal unless there is a documented, security-reviewed reason for the fallthrough. Remove legacy GPG fallback if NSG signing is the intended validation mechanism.
findings.notes: NEEDS FURTHER ANALYSIS of nsgsig-verify binary to determine what conditions produce return codes 2 and 5. Also need to examine /etc/.gnupg/ keyring contents (not present in SFLoader initramfs -- may only exist in full SFOS rootfs).

next_steps: Binary analysis of fwhttpd -- disassemble and audit multipart POST parsing for memory corruption
Reverse engineer loadfw key derivation function (fcn.00418fe8) to decrypt SFOS root filesystem
Analyze nsgsig-verify binary to understand error code semantics
Resolve SFOS VM emulation (QEMU disk geometry mismatch) to enable live testing of network-exposed services
Once SFOS rootfs extracted: map full pre-auth web attack surface (port 4444 admin, port 443 user portal)
